comments.text: This note by me+ChatGPT shows, for each $3 \le n \le 7$, existence of $n$ generators less than $p_n$, the $n$th prime, for a free multiplicative semigroup whose elements differ by at least $1$. This is basically an operationalized version of Tao's probabilistic sketch in the comments. By the Barreto-Price extension step, this shows that the inequality in the problem can fail for $x = 5-\varepsilon, 7-\varepsilon, 11-\varepsilon, 13-\varepsilon, 17-\varepsilon$ for small $\varepsilon > 0$. We think it can fail for arbitarily large $x$ but do not know how to prove.

I+ChatGPT have obtained in this note that $$ a_1 = 101/42 \approx 2.40, \quad a_2 = 367/103 \approx 3.56, \quad a_3 = 113/24 \approx 4.71 $$ generate a free multiplicative semigroup whose elements differ by at least $1$. The approach here uses tools from computational Diophantine approximation, by Matveev + LLL reduction by de Weger. By using the extension step in Barreto-Leeham, we can extend to an infinite sequence. Since $a_3 < p_3 = 5$, the primes are not everywhere minimal, which is the same version of the problem disproved by Barreto-Leeham. The eventually minimal version appears more difficult and cannot be directly tackled by this method.

It occurs to me that a probabilistic construction, together with some brute force numerics, should also work here. Numerical trial and error should be able to generate intervals of values for candidate $a_i$ (e.g., to follow Nat's example, one could try $a_1 \in [2.40,2.41]$, $a_2 \in [3.56, 3.57]$, $a_3 \in [4.71, 4.72]$ which can be shown by interval arithmetic to avoid near-collisions $|\prod_i a_i^{k_i} - \prod_j a_j^{\ell_j}| < 1$ for all $k_i, \ell_j$ below a certain threshold, say $\sum_i k_i \log a_i \leq 50$ (I am taking a total guess with this threshold), regardless of where in the interval $a_1,a_2,a_3$ are (note that this also forces $\sum_j \ell_j \log a_j$ to be bounded by $50$ up to some negligible error). To handle the remaining cases when $\sum_i k_i \log a_i > 50$, one can try the probabilistic method, choosing $a_1,a_2,a_3$ randomly; the probability of a collision for any given $k_1,k_2,k_3,\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3$ should drop off exponentially in these parameters (in particular gaining a factor like $e^{-50}$ at some point) and so hopefully one can keep the total failure probability below 1, at which point one wins. On the one hand this is not as constructive an example as the other ones, but the numerical verification may be easier and so one might be able to explore what happens at other values of $x$ than $4.71$ or $10$. EDIT: Ooh, I can set AlphaEvolve to work on this! I'll try it now. SECOND EDIT (20 mins later): For the $x=7-\varepsilon = p_4-\varepsilon$ problem, AE has already produced the candidate $$a_1 = 2.6728591026637005, a_2 = 3.733826126571687, a_3 = 4.841402115246144, a_4 := 5.999121968603969$$ which verifies the $1$-separation condition for all $\prod_{i=1}^4 a_i^{k_i} \leq e^{64.25}$ (this is about 230,000 numbers, or about $e^{12.34}$, so well below the threshold for the birthday paradox to trigger). I imagine almost any reasonable probabilistic analysis would show that some perturbation of this would show that one can break the inequality $\# \{ a_i \leq x\} \leq \pi(x)$ for $x=7-\varepsilon$ (or even $x=6$). It seems that counterexamples are in fact numerically plentiful. THIRD EDIT: It may get significantly harder to produce counterexamples as $x$ increases by such probabilistic methods. Already the birthday paradox suggests that it will be hard to randomly produce $\ggg \sqrt{x}$ values of $a_i$ in $[1,x]$ whose pairwise products $a_i a_j$ are all $1$-separated, let alone $>\pi(x)$ many such values. Possibly a hybrid approach where one first finds a (log-)algebraic construction with good separation properties for small products, followed by a probabilistic perturbation to guarantee good separation for large products as well, may work. FOURTH EDIT: Perhaps the theory of Sidon sets (and B_h[g] sets, etc.) can be used to provide good constructions?
This seems perhaps most computationally promising among the 3 approaches. I’ll try to see if we can get $n$ generators below $p_n$ for a few small $n$ by this and will report back.

Obtained $n$ generators below $p_n$ for each $3 \le n \le 7$! The case $n=7$ is becoming tricky to certify, so I choose to (arbitrarily) stop here. Noteworthy: there are more details to work through than initially expected. Luckily, with the new GPT-based workflow, tried out for the first time here, we brought it to the finish line in 44 turns and more than 8 hours from GPT's side. If I were to work alone, it would've taken much longer (likely already abandoned). Will write it up soon.

After about 20 back-and-forth prompts of asking different GPT-5.2 Pro instances to come up with, and then double-check the argument the prior instance gave autonomously, Leeham managed to get GPT-5.2 Pro to disprove this essentially fully autonomously (his only involvement being to ask the previous instance to write a LaTeX prompt for an AI to fix the proof and passing that to the next instance, until nothing wrong was identified by the model). All credit goes to him for this one. It should be classified as a section 1(a) result assuming things are correct. He asked that I have a look, and I personally think its construction is genuinely creative! Up to issues of floating point precision, it seems right and novel to me; I could not locate any prior resolution in the literature. We are waiting for Aristotle to come back with a formalisation, but in the meantime, I would appreciate others having a look here.

Congratulations to Leeham! Questions out of curiosity: * How long took the 20 rounds of pingpong? * May Leeham provide either the 20 pdfs to give us a full impression or at least some proper diagrams showing the amounts of progress in each round? One diagram might show the thinking times of 5.2 in the different rounds. * At which step was he sure to be "winning"?

Thanks for the kind words! I will be able to provide the chat links to each instance and I can potentially create a diagram showing what the workflow looked like, I will just wait for the formalisation to be finished. Obviously they will be shown in the chat links, but the thinking times ranged from probably 50 to 100 minutes and took me around 2 - 3 days from start to finish. As Kevin noted, I myself didn't instruct 5.2 Pro to do anything other than what the other instances of 5.2 Pro believed needed to be fixed, so this is still essentially autonomous.
Didn't you sometimes have connection losses in these long thinking periods (50 - 100 minutes)? I even get them rather often in 10-20 minutes "processes".

That was the case for GPT-5.2 Thinking, but Leeham has since been given access to GPT-5.2 Pro, which is much more stable over long periods. Ever since the release of GPT-5.1, the Thinking model often times out after 15 minutes, and nothing has been done to fix this... only GPT-5 Thinking remains stable in this way for the non-Pro models.

Very rarely; since using GPT-5.2 Pro on my personal account, I have had maybe 1 or 2 timeouts out of the probably 80-100 prompts, and because of the difficulty of the problems, it consistently thought for quite a while (perhaps maybe 1 or 2 instances of the 20 thought for 30 - 40 minutes).

This is perhaps the most complex arrangement of autonomy so far. Intuitively, it does speak to a real fundamental barrier in research: ideas take time to build, so it is inherently difficult for a single (say 90-minute) session to settle a big problem. Hence, one must find a way to "scale up" thinking time while still maintaining progress. In any case, congrats!

Yes, I think this speaks to a difference in methodology: one-shot attempts are unlikely to succeed currently, leading to large databases of LLM attempts on all the Erdos problems being essentially full of entirely incorrect attempts. It is clear from this case that persistence is vital.

Hmm, this looks like a nice idea, but if I'm reading it right it just disproves it for $x=10$? So this construction of an infinite sequence could still have the number of $a_i\leq x$ being $\leq \pi(x)$ for all large $x$? Is there a possibility it can be tweaked to get some measurable failure of this inequality for infinitely many $x$? (This may come under the category of number theoretic questions that fail for small examples for law of small numbers reasons, but the spirit of the question is really about the asymptotic behaviour.) To be clear, this is a nice clever construction, and something non-trivial is going on here! It would be good to push further on the theory side I think to develop this idea into a more robust construction.
I+ChatGPT have assessed and are reasonably confident that the note is correct as written.

Nice result. The numerical computations in particular need to be verified, but there does not seem to be any theoretical reason why this would not work, though as Thomas points out the proof technique relies heavily on being able to produce a counterexample in the bounded $x$ regime (in fact $x=10$ in this argument) rather than the asymptotic regime $x \ggg 1$. This changes the game considerably, and the AI proof observes that one can now convert the problem to one in Diophantine approximation. The first observation is that once one is committed to producing a counterexample at a single value of $x$, the choices of $a_i$ larger than $x$ are pretty much irrelevant; they can be constructed by a standard greedy algorithm. So now one is looking at a multiplicative semigroup generated by a bounded number of generators $\{ a_i \leq x \}$, which is expected to become exponentially sparse at infinity (cf. Størmer's theorem or Baker's theorem). But as the AI observes, one does not need the fanciest transcendence theorems such as Baker's theorem involving logs of algebraic numbers, because the $a_i$ are not required to be algebraic; instead, by taking the $a_i$ to be exponentials of algebraic numbers, one now just needs good lower bounds on Diophantine approximation by algebraic numbers, for which the most classical of bounds - Liouville's bound - is already powerful, giving lower bounds that are polynomial in the height (after extending to higher-dimensional approximation in a standard fashion), whereas to get unit separation one needs lower bounds that are at worst exponentially decaying in the height. This is a lot of room, and already guarantees unit separation for all sufficiently large products, reducing things to a finite check. (This already shows by the way that merely assuming asymptotic unit separation would not be enough to get the required bound; if the problem had any hope of a positive answer, one would need unit separation at all scales, not just the large ones.) A human at this point would perhaps balk at the significant numerical experimentation one would now have to do to find a set of generators for which the required finite number of separation conditions actually hold; but apparently an AI (prompted sufficiently many times) has much less reluctance to attempt some sort of search (possibly brute force or trial-and-error in nature). What is not currently clear is how much "room" there is here - would any reasonable set of generator choices in a reasonable algebraic number field have had a good chance to succeed? Perhaps further numerical exploration will clarify this. As this question is not directly from Erdos, but from someone in the audience at a talk he gave (see [p. 68, Er77c]) it is harder than usual to divine intent for this question, and in particular whether $x$ was intended to range over all natural numbers or only sufficiently large ones. Both formulations of the question are interesting, but for different reasons: the latter is more in the spirit of "Beurling prime number theory", but the former has the flavor of a rigidity theorem (in the spirit of Hamburger's rigidity theorem for the zeta function). Another variant which could be interesting if one requires the $a_j$ to be algebraic, which rules out the AI construction and would require one to use the more transcendental Diophantine approximation theorems if one wanted to mimic the same strategy. (But perhaps one could still win by performing an infinitesimal perturbation of the transcendental example.) Given the social media circus from past 1(a) candidates, I think we can afford to take a more measured approach with evaluating this particular submission, so that we have time for literature review, Lean formalization, and numerical exploration. I will move it to the "pending assessment" category for now.

Ok, agreed. I may be too hasty putting it as "partial result" from assuming asymptotic intent. Clearly there are a few interesting variants! I+ChatGPT is working independently and seems to obtain $3$ *rational* ($\mathbb{Q}$) generators, by a slightly different method. I'm writing it up now. This may help answer the question regarding algebraic $a_i$. (Note: it doesn't directly answer it yet, because the extension argument in the note is measure-theoretic, and doesn't seem to go though when we try to get a rational $a_i$ because the set of rational numbers has measure zero.)

Now that the situation has stabilized and we have more of a consensus as to the intent of the problem and status of the solution, I have listed the various results here under Sections 1(a), 1(b), and 1(d) of the wiki as partial results. I think the policy of placing results that have a significant probability of going viral on social media under pending assessment (Section 3) for a few days is one I will try to adopt more systematically going forward.

From what I've seen of similar papers and results concerning Beurling primes and 'generalised integers', I believe that the asymptotic question was intended (and generally when thinking of these problems a general real sequence of $a_i$ is considered, rather than algebraic numbers or integers, although of course these are subproblems). I've had a bit of a look at papers, and surprisingly can't find anything on this problem - there are a few problems which say that if the number of 'generalised integers' in $[1,x]$ are $\sim Ax$ then what kind of asymptotic on the number of 'generalised primes' $a_i\leq x$ can be obtained, but nothing (that I've found) about what happens if you just assume that the generalised integers are $1$-separated, as in this problem. It might be that even the strict inequality for all large $x$ is stronger than what Erdős (or rather the question asker, perhaps Shapiro) intended, and maybe even something like $\#\{a_i\leq x\} \leq (1+o(1))\pi(x)$ is the real spirit of the question.

Very nice! I like the process and looking forward to confirmation of the construction. I was actually trying to prove it with GPT-5.2 and didn't get far (no wonder), but managed to prove it in the case when all $a_i$ are integers. This is pretty trivial but worth mentioning. Non-integer case looked difficult.
What a coincidence! I had also tried to prove it with GPT-5.2 Pro, but it could only prove the case where all $a_i$ are integers. The non-integer case looked much more difficult.

For $s>1$, one has $\sum_{k_1,k_2,\dots} \frac{1}{(\prod_i a_i^{k_i})^s} \leq \sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{1}{n^s}$, hence $\prod_i (1-\frac{1}{a_i^s})^{-1} \leq \zeta(s)$, so on taking logarithms $\sum_i \sum_{j=1}^\infty \frac{1}{j a_i^{js}} \leq \log \zeta(s)$. This may be enough to get a Mertens type bound, but the prime number theorem type bound seems out of reach by this method.
